lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market sporting-goods firms whose external-customer proposition includes repeat team or institutional supply, equipment fitting, decoration, repair, maintenance, or rental services alongside merchandise.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure transactional stores and e-commerce sellers, captive operations, product manufacturers and wholesalers, fitness facilities, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without a recurring or repeat outsourced-service relationship.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat sales and service to schools, leagues, clubs, institutions, and consumers, generally through merchandise margin plus service, rental, repair, fitting, or decoration fees; contracts and standing accounts may exist but much revenue remains order-based.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS population mixes predominantly transactional merchandise retailers with a much smaller set of repeat team/institutional dealers and repair, fitting, decoration, or rental operators. The lens is narrowed to the latter for business-model coherence because pure retail does not supply a recurring outsourced service.

a.bridge: BLS measures industry employment and wages, not task-level AI exposure. The estimate maps high exposure to clerical, customer-service, ordering, marketing, bookkeeping, scheduling, and routine sales-support tasks, partial exposure to managers and salespeople, and little exposure to physical repair, fitting, stocking, and fulfillment. Because the qualifying firms are more service-intensive than the whole NAICS population, the industry-wide mix may overstate automatable checkout work but understate automatable quoting and account administration.
a.rationale: The May 2023 OEWS industry mix is dominated by sales occupations (64.86% of employment), including retail salespersons (48.60%) and cashiers (6.42%); office and administrative support adds 10.47%, including customer service at 2.80%, while installation, maintenance, and repair is 5.94%. AI can assist product search, quoting, customer communications, order entry, marketing, and back-office work, but most sales-floor and repair activity remains physical and interactive.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across independent team dealers, bicycle/outdoor repair shops, and rental operators, linking payroll by role to task-level GenAI and workflow-automation trials, would materially change the range.
caveats: OEWS covers all employer establishments in NAICS 459110 rather than the narrowed recurring-service subset and excludes self-employed owners.
Occupation shares are employment-weighted, while the primitive is wage-weighted; published industry mean wages inform but do not fully identify task-level wage exposure.
Current automation already embedded in POS, e-commerce, and inventory systems is not separately measured.

rho.bridge: BLS national occupation projections and one large retailer's disclosed optimization are implementation signals rather than a five-year adoption rate for qualifying LMM firms. Cashier and customer-service employment declines are attributed partly to self-service, online sales, and automation; these signals are translated into a bounded implementation share, with a downward adjustment for small-firm systems, data, change-management, and service-quality constraints.
rho.rationale: BLS projects cashier employment down 10% and customer-service representatives down 5% from 2024 to 2034, while retail sales employment is essentially flat, indicating selective rather than wholesale labor substitution. DICK'S disclosed eliminating customer-support positions and streamlining its cost structure, demonstrating implementation in the sector, but independent operators have fewer integration resources and cannot remove accountable human service from fitting, repair, and institutional relationships.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year cohort data from POS/ERP vendors serving independent sporting-goods dealers, together with audited pre/post labor hours for AI customer service, quoting, purchasing, and inventory deployments, would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Employment projections are national and ten-year, not direct five-year implementation measures.
DICK'S is much larger and better capitalized than the target population.
Some projected employment change reflects e-commerce and self-checkout rather than AI.

e.bridge: OEWS counts workers, not eligible firms. The estimate uses the presence of repair (5.94% of industry employment), counter/rental clerk (0.62%), and service-sales (0.45%) roles as indicators of service-bearing establishments, then allows a wider upper bound for team and institutional dealers whose recurring account work is performed by general sales staff. Employment concentration at multi-location retailers can bias the mapping in either direction.
e.rationale: Most establishments in the code sell goods transactionally and fail the recurring outsourced-service lens. A minority of bicycle, ski, golf, team-sports, and outdoor dealers provide repeat repair, fitting, rental, decoration, or institutional procurement relationships that can qualify, but the industry employment mix suggests this is a narrow subset.
e.change_evidence: A stratified establishment census using merchant websites, NAICS microdata, and interviews with NSGA/SFIA members to identify recurring contracts, repair/rental revenue, and institutional accounts would directly estimate eligible firm share.
caveats: Occupation presence does not reveal whether service revenue is recurring or whether a firm has normalized EBITDA of $1–10 million.
Team dealers may be classified partly in wholesale trade or apparel decoration, creating boundary leakage.
The frozen n input is missing, so eligible count cannot be derived from this share in the packet.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding U.S. employer-business owners were age 55 or older, and BizBuySell reports realized transactions for independent retail businesses, but neither measures five-year control transfers among eligible sporting-goods firms. The estimate translates broad succession pressure and observable retail liquidity into a five-year probability, reducing for family/internal transfers, closures, firms outside the EBITDA band, and businesses too owner-dependent to transfer.
s5.rationale: Owner age creates meaningful succession supply, and BizBuySell's retail data confirm an active market with a 2025 median sale price of $273,647 and median 165 days on market. However, those transactions are generally much smaller than the target EBITDA band, and eligibility requires both a qualifying service model and transferable operations.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal and mandate data for sporting-goods dealers from Axial, GF Data, DealStats, and industry-specialist brokers, matched to owner age and EBITDA, would materially refine the five-year transfer rate.
caveats: The Census age statistic is for responding owners across all employer industries and is dated to data year 2018.
BizBuySell is a marketplace sample, not a census, and its median retail transaction is below the target LMM band.
Owner age is not the same as intent to sell, and qualifying control transfers exclude closures and internal reorganizations.

q.bridge: DICK'S disclosures describe retail competition and pricing transparency, not the retained share of AI benefits for LMM service-bearing dealers. The estimate applies those competitive conditions to fixed-price merchandise and fee-based services, with greater retention where labor savings improve turnaround or service quality and lower retention where schools, leagues, or consumers rebid and compare prices.
q.rationale: Merchandise and posted service prices do not contractually pass labor savings through immediately, allowing initial retention. Over renewals, real-time price comparison, direct-to-consumer vendors, online sellers, and fragmented competition pressure prices and service fees, while some value is shared through faster quotes, better availability, and service levels.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples, bid histories, gross-margin bridges, and cohort pricing from independent team dealers and repair/rental operators before and after workflow automation would identify actual pass-through and renewal repricing.
caveats: A large public retailer's competitive environment may not match local institutional accounts or specialist repair niches.
The estimate separates demand volume and implementation difficulty but cannot fully isolate AI savings from merchandising and procurement gains.
Customer concentration and rebid cadence vary substantially across schools, clubs, and consumers.

d5.bridge: SFIA measures annual sporting-goods industry and wholesale-sales growth, not constant-price demand for the narrowed service basket. The estimate uses reported 2025 industry growth of 3.4%, record participation, and 3.7% wholesale growth as positive directional demand signals, then removes assumed price effects, avoids compounding one strong year mechanically, and narrows toward modest real five-year growth. Manufacturer coverage and event-driven demand may overstate dealer-service growth, while participation-linked repair and team demand may be steadier.
d5.rationale: Sports and fitness participation is at record levels, SFIA reported sporting-goods industry growth of 3.4% in 2025 and wholesale sales up 3.7%, and major U.S. events may support engagement. Against that, sporting goods remain discretionary and exposed to affordability, tariffs, weather, and category cycles, supporting a near-flat to modest-growth constant-price range.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of inflation-adjusted same-customer order volumes, repair tickets, rental days, and team participation by category from dealer POS systems and SFIA participation microdata would materially change the range.
caveats: SFIA sales growth is not explicitly constant-price and covers manufacturers and wholesale categories beyond the lens.
DICK'S comparable sales include price, mix, digital subscriptions, and media revenue.
World Cup and Olympics effects may be temporary and category-specific.

o.bridge: BLS and DICK'S describe channel and occupation changes rather than the year-five operator-required share of the narrowed basket. The estimate treats online sales, self-service checkout, vendor direct-to-consumer channels, and AI-assisted product discovery as substitution routes, while preserving operator need for accountable institutional fulfillment, customization, repair, fitting, returns, and local service recovery.
o.rationale: Pure product discovery and checkout can migrate to self-service, marketplaces, or brands, but the qualifying lens deliberately centers services whose physical execution and accountability remain local. Retail-sales jobs are projected essentially flat even as cashiers decline 10%, consistent with channel automation changing tasks more than eliminating the operator for complex or service-bearing purchases.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level churn studies comparing institutional dealers with direct-to-team platforms, plus longitudinal channel shares for repair, rental, customization, and fitting transactions, would materially refine operator displacement.
caveats: BLS projections cover occupations across industries and do not isolate sporting-goods service transactions.
Vendor direct-to-consumer capabilities and remote fitting or customization technology could improve faster than assumed.
The estimate applies to the narrowed service-bearing subset, not the full NAICS retail basket.

narrative.executive_view: Sporting-goods retail is mostly outside the recurring outsourced-service lens. The coherent target is the narrow subset of team and institutional dealers and specialist repair, rental, fitting, or decoration operators. Within that subset, AI can reduce quoting, order-entry, customer-service, marketing, purchasing, and administrative work, but physical service and relationship accountability limit substitution.
narrative.ai_impact: The largest practical opportunities are product and inventory search, team-order configuration, quote generation, customer communications, order-status handling, scheduling, purchasing suggestions, marketing content, and routine bookkeeping. Physical fitting, repair, customization, stocking, loss prevention, and exception handling remain human-heavy, and the industry's dominant retail-sales workforce caps wage-weighted exposure.
narrative.value_capture: Posted merchandise and service prices permit initial retention of labor savings, especially when automation improves turnaround or availability. Over time, transparent online comparison, direct-to-consumer brands, competitive bids, and fragmented local competition should share part of the benefit with customers through lower prices or better service.
narrative.firm_availability: Only a small share of sporting-goods retailers appears eligible because ordinary product sales are transactional. Succession pressure is real across small business and a retail resale market exists, but public marketplace transactions skew far below the target EBITDA range and owner-dependent shops may not transfer cleanly.
narrative.demand_durability: Record sports participation and recent industry growth support broadly stable to modestly growing real demand. The service-bearing basket should retain a local operator for repair, fitting, customization, rental, and institutional fulfillment, although product discovery, checkout, and some team ordering can move to self-service or vendor-direct channels.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main uncertainty is population fit: recurring-service operators are a small, poorly measured slice of a broad retail code and may be classified elsewhere. Tariffs, discretionary spending, weather, category fashion, inventory shrink, e-commerce, and vendor direct-to-consumer strategies can pressure volumes and margins. Evidence on LMM deal flow and actual AI labor savings is especially thin.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat institutional relationships and physical repair, fitting, rental, and customization preserve an accountable operator while administrative workflows offer selective AI savings.
narrative.principal_weakness: The eligible recurring-service subset is small and not directly measured inside a predominantly transactional retail NAICS code.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 459110 Sporting Goods Retailers
sources.supports: Industry employment of 302,960; occupation shares including sales 64.86%, retail salespersons 48.60%, cashiers 6.42%, office and administrative support 10.47%, customer service 2.80%, counter/rental clerks 0.62%, and installation, maintenance, and repair 5.94%.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Cashiers
sources.supports: Cashier employment is projected to decline 10% from 2024 to 2034; BLS attributes the decline to technology such as self-service checkout and increasing online sales.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Customer Service Representatives
sources.supports: Customer-service-representative employment is projected to decline 5% from 2024 to 2034; duties include complaints, orders, questions, returns, billing, and account changes.

sources.title: DICK'S Sporting Goods, Inc. Form 10-K for fiscal year ended February 1, 2025
sources.supports: Comparable sales rose 5.2% in fiscal 2024 after 2.6% in 2023; the company disclosed a customer-support-center workforce optimization, intense fragmented competition, real-time price comparison, online and vendor-direct channels, and continued digital and store investments.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey, data year 2018, found 51% of responding U.S. employer-business owners were age 55 or older, with explicit limits on representativeness.

sources.title: Retail Business Valuation Multiples and Financial Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reported independent retail-business transactions had a 2025 median sale price of $273,647; the broader benchmark reports median revenue of $720,000, median owner earnings of $131,498, and median 165 days on market.

sources.title: Sports and Fitness Industry Association Press Releases
sources.supports: SFIA reported 3.4% sporting-goods industry growth in 2025, record sports and fitness participation, and 3.7% year-over-year growth in wholesale sporting-goods sales to $130 billion, while identifying tariffs and costs as constraints.
